title: AEE 2651: Find Out How to Use "Find" in Three Different Ways

sentences.en: This is an All Ears English podcast, episode 2651.
Find out how to use find in three different ways.
Welcome to the All Ears English podcast.
Downloaded more than 200 million times.
Are you feeling stuck with your English?
We'll show you how to become fearless and fluent by focusing on connection, not perfection.
With your American hosts, Lindsay McMahon, the English adventurer, and Michelle Kaplan, the New York Radio Girl.
Coming to you from Colorado and New York City, USA.
To get real-time transcripts right on your phone and create your personalized vocabulary list, try the All Ears English app for iOS and Android.
Start your seven-day free trial at allearsenglish.com, board slash app.
When someone asks you how did you find this podcast, are they asking about your opinion, how you located it, or something else?
Today, let's put an end to this common error in English.
Have you ever been in an English conversation where you understood everything, but when it was your turn to speak, the words disappeared?
You've learned more sophisticated phrases, but in the moment you fall back on simple, safe words.
That's exactly why we created our brand new online course, Active Vocabulary.
This course helps you move your vocabulary from passive recognition to active use.
So the words are there when you need them, in those important moments of your life.
Plus, when you become a student, you get invited to the Weekly Open Conversation Club to practice your speaking live.
Save up to $100, but the offer ends on Sunday.
Go now to allearsenglish.com slash vocabulary to get the special deal.
That's allearsenglish.com slash v-o-c-a-b-u-l-a-r-y.
Hey Michelle, how's it going today?
Pretty well, Lindsay.
Lindsay, how did you find your current house?
How did I find my current house?
Good question.
I think we found it on Redfin, because even though we had a realtor, I feel like nowadays the people looking for the house tend to find the house and they send it to their realtor.
Yes, I totally agree.
Yes, well, yeah, that has happened to me for sure before, where I'm sending things to the realtor.
Yes, funny.
Well, it kind of makes sense as you sort of know better what you want, but then I'm kind of like, well, jeez, that's part of the realtor's job.
What are we paying for?
Yeah, yeah.
So it's interesting.
So but today we're going to talk about the verb to find and how I can be used in many different ways.
So we spoke about this briefly on another episode and we got the idea to expand on it because it's really useful and common.
And Lindsay, what is like, you've had this conversation with listeners before.
Tell us about it.
Many times I've met with listeners.
We have our meet and greets, our meet and greet contests.
We have opportunities to meet our listeners one on one.
Over at Open Conversation Club, I get to meet with one or two listeners in the hot seat.
And I often will ask our listeners or students, how did you find the podcast?
And they will say, great, I love it.
But Michelle, what is the problem there?
Well, you were actually trying to ask them how did they locate it?
Locate it.
Right, where did that, how did somebody send it to them?
Did they find it by doing a search?
Right, you're not asking about how they liked it.
Exactly. And it's actually really tricky if you think about it because if I had said, how do you find the podcast, then that would their answer would be correct.
Right? Yeah.
How do you find this?
How do you find that?
Although it's a little less common, I think, in American English.
But I said, how did you find it?
So that points to how did you locate it?
Well, I will also say though, it depends on the context.
Like if you said to them, how did you find it?
And you are talking about a specific episode or something.
But then that might mean, how did you like it?
Yeah, so this is actually more tricky.
Or if I recommended a restaurant to you, you went on Saturday night.
How did you find that restaurant?
Yeah, that actually could mean two different things.
But because I know that you, I recommended it to you.
That's how you found it.
That's how you located it.
Now I'm asking you about your opinion on that restaurant.
Oh man, this is tough.
Yeah, so we're just opening a can of worms here, Michelle.
So now we got to dive into the can of worms.
We certainly are.
Before we do, we want to remind you guys to hit follow.
We're everywhere listening to the All Years English podcast.
Never miss anything from us.
Exactly. So let's break this down for our listeners.
If you guys just got really confused, don't panic, it's okay.
We're here for you.
Let's break it down into the buckets.
We've got three different buckets.
We've got, what's the first bucket, Michelle?
The first bucket is to locate or get.
So for example, I found this book on the floor of the bathroom.
Okay, yes.
So I found it, right?
Jason found a job.
I'm so excited.
Okay. Or I couldn't find you.
Were you in the store?
Okay. Then the second bucket is the think experience opinion bucket.
Right? This is about your opinion on something, right?
Yeah. This is what our listeners thought I was asking, right?
Yeah. So she found the concert to be extremely boring.
Or I find her super funny.
She's my favorite comedian.
It's your opinion.
It's what you think about them.
You're evaluating them.
Yeah. Yeah.
And then the third one is a realization, okay?
So I find that I work best when there's a little bit of background noise.
Or after a few months, I found I didn't really want to live in this city anymore.
Yeah, that could be kind of like a sub meaning of the think experience opinion, but it's like it happened in a moment, right?
Right. Right.
You're having an actual realization moment.
But let's talk a little bit about the grammar here.
So if you follow find with an A, right?
Oh, sorry.
No, no, no, no.
If you follow find with an object and an adjective, it's generally an opinion.
So for example, I find my coworkers friendly.
Yes, that's an opinion.
That is not locating them.
Where if you follow find with a that clause, it becomes something you've realized.
I find that I sleep best in the pitch black dark.
Oh, I've been dealing with this lately.
Been dealing with a lot of light pollution because we just move back into our apartment in the city and there's a huge streetlight like right below our bedroom window.
And it's making me crazy.
I have to wear an eye mask.
Yeah, I tried that last night, but they're so annoying on your face.
I love them.
Really? Okay.
You have to give me a recommendation for a brand or something.
I could send you the one I have.
Okay. I like it.
Okay. Realization, right?
I mean, you might not always say that.
I mean, you still might say I find I sleep best, right?
But if you add to that, then it's definitely something you've realized.
Yes. Okay.
And then the last one.
If you follow find with a person or a place or an object, it's generally about location.
So I found my keys in my bag.
Yeah. And be careful because it's not, we're not saying it's really about tense of the verb so much, right?
Because you could also, I mean, it's not so much about the tense.
It's about what comes after.
So the content, right?
And guys, these are just general rules, but it doesn't always work out that way.
So you really, to me, you have to really think about the context, but we'll talk more in a minute.
Tricky. Starting your own business is a dream for so many people.
It's exciting, but it can be overwhelming.
I know because I've done it.
But Shopify makes it simpler.
All the tools you need to launch are already included and ready from day one.
Shopify's templates and AI tools get you a stunning site up and running fast.
No coding needed.
This means that you're free to create your brand in a way that reflects your company and your values.
Shopify checkout means more customers actually buy and returning ones can do it in a single click.
I remember so many long days and nights when I started all ears English, trying to figure out how to make my website work.
But Shopify's built-in AI assistant sidekick is ready to help you build, troubleshoot, and keep moving if questions come up.
All you need is the idea.
Shopify handles the rest.
Start your free trial at Shopify.com slash AEE.
Again, start your free trial at Shopify.com slash AEE.
One more time, get your free trial at shopify.com slash AEE.
All right, Michelle, it's time to talk more.
Let's do it.
Here we go.
I lost my spot, but I'm going.
Oh, there we go.
So I found my spot.
The interesting part is how it can be a location, but also an opinion.
Like we talked about, how did you find the seats?
It could mean were they good or if you say how did you find the seats?
It might be how did you locate them?
Did you look at a map?
Did somebody help you?
That's so tricky.
So here you really have to know who's asking you this.
What did they know previously about you and the seats and its context, right?
Yeah, right.
Exactly. Tell us more.
So well, we're going to play a little game now, Wednesday.
So are we asking for opinion or location?
Here we go.
How did you find those earrings?
Oh, they were in my room.
So clearly here we're looking for a location, right?
We've lost something.
How did you find the test?
It was really hard.
Yeah, like if I'm saying how did you find the test?
It sounds like we both know about the test.
I don't know why I would be asking how you located the test.
I mean what my teacher put it on the table, like I don't know.
The only scenario I would think if you're looking for a room.
Saturday morning SAT and there's no one in the hallway.
You're wondering where's my test center, right?
Like for the IELTS exam.
But yeah.
So context plus gut feeling a little bit here, right?
Yeah, yeah.
Or sometimes we might use the continuous tense.
So we would say how are you finding something?
Or I'm finding something X.
So we'll do some examples in that and just an opinion.
Would that ever be used for location, do you think?
It really wouldn't.
Yeah, it really wouldn't because you can't really be in the process of finding something.
You can be in the process of searching for something if you lose your keys.
But you wouldn't really say like what's the method you're using for finding your keys, right?
Right. That's a little weird.
Exactly. Yeah, so it would be more for the opinion piece.
So I mean we would use present and continuous if the situation is still going on.
Or you're still discovering how you feel.
So maybe the change is still occurring or maybe it's temporary.
So for example, how are you finding the class so far?
Yeah, like what do you think of the class so far, right?
That's opinion.
How is she finding married life?
What are her opinions on married life?
Or I'm finding this position to be different than I expected.
Okay, good.
So context, context, context.
That's all we could say guys.
And knowing what they know about what they're asking you about, right?
Like get in the person's head, right?
Yep, exactly.
Where are they coming from?
And then you'll be sure to know what they're saying.
So let's do a role play here.
We are at the store looking for a cake.
Yay, fun.
I know, fun, right?
Okay, did you find the cake?
No, I just saw them there yesterday.
Well, maybe they sold out.
Maybe. I'm finding this grocery store isn't what it used to be.
Yeah, they never seem to have enough stuff.
How did you find the other place from last week?
It was huge, but they definitely had more things in stock.
Okay. All right, tricky here.
So did you find the cake is the first thing you asked me?
So is this about location or is this about opinion?
Yeah, it's about location.
Definitely, right?
It wouldn't make sense if it's an opinion question to say did you find the cake?
Right, you'd have to say how did you find the cake?
Right, what else?
And then I said maybe they sold out and you said maybe.
I'm finding this grocery store isn't what it used to be.
So opinion or realizing something is coming to believe, right?
Yes, and then you said, yeah, they seem to never have enough stuff.
How did you find the other place from last week?
And this one is tricky because I read this at first as like, oh, we're asking about location.
Like how did you find it?
Like who told you about it or where it's located?
But then I thought maybe we're asking about opinion based on the answer.
That one actually could technically be either, don't you think?
It could, but in this context, we're kind of imagining.
I know maybe that we already touched on this.
You know that I went to another place, but I didn't tell you my opinion of it, right?
So this is really like, it depends how much the other person knows about the situation.
Yeah, we are talking about opinions on grocery stores and this thread, this immediate piece.
So that's the clue right there, but that's tricky.
I mean, that shows you how much we really need to pay attention here.
Yeah, definitely.
But yeah, this is tricky, but really good and useful because you're going to hear this all the time.
So don't be afraid of it.
Just give it a try.
Take a couple of the basic ones and start with those, right?
So yeah, guys, check out episode 2630.
That was Don't Have the Heart to Do It, What to Say in English.
Lindsay, take away for today.
Wow, take away.

I love when we get into a topic that seems very straightforward, but it's actually a little bit more nuanced because it gets us into connection even more because what this is asking us to do is really think about who we're speaking to and what they know about you, what they're likely to be saying to you in that moment.
Yeah, so pay special attention to this.
The next time you hear the verb find, really think hard.
Are we asking about location?
Are we asking about opinion or realization, right?
Good stuff.
I'm glad we talked about this today.
And guys, I hope you have found that you enjoyed this episode.
Which one was that, Michelle?
That's opinion or...
Yeah, exactly.
All right, good stuff.
All right, you have a good day, Michelle.
All right, you too.
Bye. Okay, bye.
Subscribe now to make sure you don't miss anything.
See you next time.
